query: effect of bromocriptine on prolactin levels

retrieved_docs.title: Bromocriptine -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Bromocriptine, an ergot alkaloid derivative classified as a dopamine D2 agonist, is a medication used to manage type 2 diabetes, Parkinson disease, acromegaly, and pituitary prolactinomas. Bromocriptine finds utility in off-label applications for peripartum cardiomyopathy and neuroleptic malignant syndrome.

retrieved_docs.title: Bromocriptine -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the indications for bromocriptine in managing type 2 diabetes, Parkinson disease, acromegaly, and pituitary prolactinomas. Implement appropriate dosage adjustments for bromocriptine based on individual patient characteristics and therapeutic response. Select bromocriptine as an appropriate treatment option based on patient-specific factors and clinical guidelines. Collaborate with interprofessional healthcare teams to ensure coordinated care, optimize treatment, and adjust interventions as necessary. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Bromocriptine -- Administration -- Specific Patient Populations
retrieved_docs.content: Bromocriptine has also been used to manage persistent galactorrhea following breast augmentation surgery and hyperprolactinemia and galactorrhea caused by antipsychotic medications. The American Academy of Breastfeeding Medicine outlines the dopamine agonists indicated for treating galactorrhea that persists despite other interventions. While cabergoline is preferred for its favorable adverse effect profile, bromocriptine serves as an alternative if cabergoline is unavailable. However, the use of bromocriptine for lactation suppression is discouraged due to the elevated risk of maternal stroke, seizures, cardiovascular disorders, death, and potentially psychosis. [27] [28]

retrieved_docs.title: Galactorrhea -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: The mainstay of hyperprolactinemia is treatment with bromocriptine or cabergoline. These are dopamine agonists which act on the D2 receptors present on the lactotrophs and inhibit prolactin production. The doses for bromocriptine and cabergoline are 2.5-15 mg once daily and cabergoline 0.25-1 mg twice weekly, respectively. They are effective at normalizing prolactin levels as well as shrinking the size of the tumor. Side effects include nausea, vomiting, orthostatic hypotension, drowsiness, and headaches. Cardiac valvulopathy has been reported with high doses of cabergoline, and it is recommended to get an annual echocardiogram in patients on more than 3.5 mg/week.

retrieved_docs.title: Schizophrenia -- Toxicity and Adverse Effect Management -- Prolactin Elevation
retrieved_docs.content: High-potency FGAs and some SGAs (risperidone, paliperidone, and amisulpride) have a greater propensity to cause prolactin elevation. This is due to dopamine D 2 receptor antagonism in the anterior pituitary, leading to disruptions in the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis and symptoms such as breast enlargement, galactorrhea, sexual dysfunction, and menstrual irregularities. Management strategies include lowering the dosage of the current antipsychotic medication, switching to an antipsychotic medication with a lower risk of increasing prolactin levels, or considering the addition of bromocriptine, a dopamine agonist. [35] If symptoms such as headache and vision changes are present in concert with elevated prolactin levels 4 times the upper limit of normal, assessment for possible pituitary tumors is warranted.

retrieved_docs.title: Prolactinoma -- Treatment / Management -- Medical Therapy
retrieved_docs.content: Bromocriptine is preferred during pregnancy if needed due to more available data than cabergoline. It is also cheaper but has more side effects than cabergoline, like nausea, vomiting, nasal stuffiness, and postural hypotension. It is started at 1.25 mg at bedtime or after dinner daily for one week, then increased to 1.25 mg 2 times a day (after breakfast and dinner). The dose should be increased every 4 weeks if the prolactin level is not normalized up to a maximum dose of 5 mg two times a day.  If bromocriptine is unsuccessful, cabergoline should be started. Treatment with dopamine agonist should be tapered and stopped if prolactin level is normal and the tumor is not visible in MRI after at least two years of treatment.

retrieved_docs.title: Bromocriptine -- Administration -- Specific Patient Populations
retrieved_docs.content: Breastfeeding considerations: Bromocriptine is generally not recommended during breastfeeding due to its lactation-suppressing effects. In some cases, a daily dose of 2.5 mg for 3 days has been utilized to reduce excessive milk production. Although the drug is undetectable in breast milk at this dosage and adverse reactions have not been reported, the safety of this practice is not fully established. Cases of mothers successfully breastfeeding while being treated with bromocriptine for amenorrhea-galactorrhea syndrome or prolactinoma during pregnancy and lactation have been documented.

retrieved_docs.title: Prolactinoma -- Treatment / Management -- Laboratory
retrieved_docs.content: Patients can have very high prolactin levels; however, when measured, they can be reported as falsely low due to a phenomenon called the “Hook effect.” When there is a suspicion, serial dilution of the serum sample and re-measuring the prolactin levels will be helpful.

retrieved_docs.title: Hyperpituitarism -- Treatment / Management -- Pharmacological Therapy
retrieved_docs.content: Prolactinomas are the only pituitary adenomas in which long-term pharmacological therapy (eg, bromocriptine and cabergoline) is satisfactory. Unless there is an acute threat, pharmacologic management should always be preferred to surgical intervention. Dopamine agonists suppress prolactin effectively and decrease serum prolactin levels. Furthermore, they reduce galactorrhea, recover the gonadal function, and have also been found to cause tumor shrinkage. Medications can also include the use of somatostatin analogs and competitive receptor antagonists. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Galactorrhea -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Galactorrhea is nonlactational production of milk. Prolactin, estrogens, and TRH all play a role in the production of milk from the breast. The condition is often managed by an interprofessional team that consists of a radiologist, endocrinologist, neurosurgeon, neurologist, and an internist. The galactorrhea associated with hyperprolactinemia is often associated with symptoms of hypogonadism (decreased libido, erectile dysfunction, and menstrual irregularities) and thus a thorough medical history and physical exam are needed. Serosanguinous discharge from the breast should prompt further evaluation for breast pathologies. Inquiring of the patient about their current medications is vital. In most cases, treatment with dopamine agonists (cabergoline or bromocriptine) normalizes prolactin levels and results in shrinkage of the tumor. (Level V)

retrieved_docs.title: Bromocriptine -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Bromocriptine is an ergot alkaloid derivative in the dopamine D2 agonist class of drugs used to manage various conditions, including type 2 diabetes, Parkinson disease, acromegaly, and pituitary prolactinomas.

retrieved_docs.title: Bromocriptine -- Administration -- Adult Dosages
retrieved_docs.content: Treatment for the following disorders must be individualized based on a patient's unique characteristics. However, the recommended dosages for the following conditions are mentioned below. Hyperprolactinemia : An initial dosage of 1.25 to 2.50 mg daily, which may be increased to a maximum maintenance dosage of 5 mg daily. [26] Acromegaly : An initial dosage of 1.25 to 2.50 mg daily, with incremental increases of 2.50 mg until desired growth hormone blood concentrations are achieved. Maintenance dosages typically range from 7.50 to 30.0 mg daily. [11] [10] Parkinson disease: The initial dosage is typically 2.50 mg daily, with increments of 2.50 mg based on tolerance and effectiveness. The goal is to use the lowest effective dose to achieve symptomatic control. A low dose is considered to be less than 30 mg daily, while a high dose ranges from 31 to 100 mg daily. [25] [13] [20]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Prolactin -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Regardless of the etiology of excess prolactin, infertility ensues in both sexes due to GnRH inhibition by prolactin, as well as galactorrhea or the inappropriate flow of breast milk in men and women. Dopamine agonist medications such as bromocriptine and cabergoline are treatment options for prolactin excess regardless of its etiology. It is important to know that hypothyroidism can present with galactorrhea. As mentioned before, TRH has a stimulatory effect on prolactin; thus, with low thyroid hormone in hypothyroidism, the negative feedback on TRH is removed, allowing for excessive prolactin release and galactorrhea.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Prolactin -- Cellular Level
retrieved_docs.content: Thyrotropin-releasing hormone (TRH) and dopamine are both released by the hypothalamus and have activity in modulating lactotophic activity. Dopamine is tonically (ie, continuous) secreted by dopaminergic neurons that project from the arcuate nucleus of the hypothalamus into the anterior pituitary gland via the infundibulum. This pathway is called the tuberoinfundibular pathway. The dopamine released at the terminal buttons of the nerves acts on lactotrophic cells through D2 receptors, modulating intracellular signaling and inhibiting prolactin synthesis. In the absence of pregnancy (ie, high estrogen) or lactation in sexually mature females, prolactin is constitutively inhibited by dopamine, and the effect of dopamine trumps the effect of minimal stimulatory effects of TRH. Additionally, prolactin has negative feedback on its own production by stimulating the release of dopamine in the hypothalamus. Medications that antagonize dopamine production (ie, antipsychotics) or masses that compress the infundibulum disrupt the basal inhibition of prolactin synthesis, resulting in hyperprolactinemia. Conversely, medications that are dopamine agonists, such as bromocriptine or cabergoline, will inhibit prolactin secretion and can be used to treat pathologies associated with hyperprolactinemia. Lastly, the decidua layer of the placenta has also been found to synthesize prolactin. This process is independent of dopamine regulation. [8] [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Prolactinoma -- Treatment / Management -- Medical Therapy
retrieved_docs.content: Cabergoline and bromocriptine are two commonly used dopamine agonists. Pergolide is withdrawn from the market due to concerns about valvular heart disease, and quinagolide is not available in the United States. Dopamine agonists suppress the synthesis and release of prolactin and lactotroph cellular proliferation, causing shrinkage of the tumor. They can cause nausea and dizziness.

retrieved_docs.title: Bromocriptine -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Bromocriptine is a dopamine receptor agonist with selective agonist activity on D2 dopamine receptors while simultaneously acting as a partial antagonist for D1 dopamine receptors. [20] Dopamine agonism has variable effects depending on the target tissue. In Parkinson disease, bromocriptine binds directly to striatal dopamine D2 receptors, stimulating locomotion and attenuating the bradykinetic symptoms caused by the degeneration of dopaminergic nigrostriatal neurons. [21] [13] Similarly, this agonistic effect on the D2 receptors of anterior pituitary lactotrophic cells inhibits prolactin exocytosis and gene expression, thereby reducing the adverse effects of hyperprolactinemia, particularly in the case of pituitary prolactinoma.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 2608
retrieved_docs.content: Bromocriptine mesylate and kabergolin are contraindicated in patients with hypertension (فرط ضغط الدم). In contrast, several agents may be used to treat this condition: lercanidipine, terazosin hydrochloride, terazosin hydrochloride anhydrous, and torasemide each have therapeutic potential for lowering blood pressure in hypertension. Moreover, hypertension is linked to a broader category of autonomic nervous system disorders (اضطرابات الجهاز العصبي المستقل), highlighting the role of dysregulated autonomic regulation in the development of elevated arterial pressure. Recognizing both the medications that should be avoided and those that can be employed, together with the underlying autonomic mechanisms, is essential for safe and effective patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 325
retrieved_docs.content: Antithyroid preparations are pharmacologic agents that counteract excess thyroid hormone production and are employed in the treatment of hyperthyroidism (فَرْطُ الدَّرَقِيَّةِ). By inhibiting the synthesis of thyroid hormones, these medications help normalize elevated metabolic activity, alleviate symptoms such as tachycardia, weight loss, and tremor, and reduce the risk of complications associated with uncontrolled hyperthyroidism. Effective use of antithyroid preparations is therefore a central component of managing patients with فَرْطُ الدَّرَقِيَّةِ.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 1 -- Community 90
retrieved_docs.content: Bromocriptine mesylate, bromodiphenhydramine, chlormethiazol, kabergolin, pergolid, piribedil, pramipexol, and tolcapon are all agents that may be used to treat Parkinson’s disease. In clinical practice, Parkinson’s disease is frequently associated with اضطرابات اكتئابية (depressive disorders) and رعاش (tremor), highlighting the importance of addressing both motor and non‑motor symptoms when selecting therapeutic strategies. The inclusion of multiple pharmacologic options reflects the heterogeneous nature of Parkinson’s disease and the need for individualized treatment plans that can alleviate tremor, improve motor function, and potentially mitigate associated depressive symptoms.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 2 -- Community 823
retrieved_docs.content: Bromocriptine mesylate and cabergoline are contraindicated in patients with فرط ضغط الدم (hypertension), indicating that these dopamine‑agonist agents should be avoided in this condition. In contrast, a broad spectrum of calcium‑channel‑blocking agents is recognized as therapeutic for hypertension. The dihydropyridine derivative class, a subgroup of selective calcium channel blockers with primarily vascular effects, includes clevidipine, felodipin, isradipine, lacidipine, lercanidipine, levamlodipine, manidipine, mepirodipine, nifedipine, nikardipine, nilvadipine, nisoldipine and nitrendipine; felodipin is specifically identified as a member of this class. Additionally, the α‑blockers terazosin hydrochloride and terazosin hydrochloride anhydrous are listed among agents suitable for managing hypertension. Mibefradil is categorized separately as an “Other selective calcium channel blocker with mainly vascular effects” within the ATC system, further illustrating the hierarchical organization of vascular‑acting calcium channel blockers. Autonomic nervous system disorders (اضطرابات الجهاز العصبي المستقل) are noted to have a broader relationship with hypertension, underscoring the role of autonomic dysregulation in the pathophysiology of elevated blood pressure. Together, these relationships delineate which agents are appropriate or inappropriate for hypertension treatment and clarify the pharmacologic classification of vascular‑acting calcium channel blockers.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 1396
retrieved_docs.content: Prochlorperazine edisylate and chlorpromazine hydrochloride are both classified as phenothiazine compounds, indicating that they belong to the same chemical class of antipsychotic and antiemetic agents. This shared classification reflects common pharmacologic properties inherent to phenothiazine drugs, such as dopamine receptor antagonism, which underlies their therapeutic use in managing psychosis, nausea, and related conditions. Recognizing that both medications are phenothiazines helps clinicians understand their similar mechanisms of action, potential side effect profiles, and considerations for patient care when selecting or monitoring treatment within this drug class.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 3 -- Community 1647
retrieved_docs.content: Bromocriptine mesylate and kabergolin (cabergoline) are contraindicated in patients with فرط ضغط الدم (hypertension), indicating that these agents should be avoided in this condition. Several antihypertensive medications may be employed to manage hypertension, including the calcium‑channel blockers lacidipine, levamlodipine, manidipine, nifedipin (nifedipine) and nitrendipin (nitrendipine), as well as the α1‑adrenergic antagonists terazosin hydrochloride and terazosin hydrochloride anhydrous. In addition to its pharmacologic considerations, فرط ضغط الدم is linked to اضطرابات الجهاز العصبي المستقل (autonomic nervous system disorders), underscoring the broader physiological context in which hypertension occurs and the importance of comprehensive patient assessment and treatment planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 2 -- Community 1178
retrieved_docs.content: Parkinson’s disease is a neurodegenerative disorder that is clinically associated with tremor (رعاش). A range of pharmacologic agents have been identified as potential treatments for Parkinson’s disease, including the dopamine‑agonist bromocriptine mesylate, bromodiphenhydramine, kabergolin, pergolid, piribedil, and pramipexol, as well as the agents chlormethiazol and tolcapon. These medications may help mitigate motor symptoms such as tremor and improve overall patient care.

Hormones form a higher‑order group that includes thyroid preparations such as dextrothyroxine sodium, thyroxin and triiodothyronine (T4, T3); these preparations regulate basal metabolic rate, growth and development, and thyreostatic agents have a direct therapeutic relationship with the thyroid gland.
